speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four, and the survivor is Mr. Victor Dortheimer. Before the tape break, you were describing when SS officers came to the factory, and how they were wined and dined. Was there any other instances when officers came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was. You going? This was a fact. Then came the [? Goth ?] with an SS man, Grun. This was Austrian. And [? Goth ?] was in factory, and the Grun went to camp. Because this was a new factory. And suddenly, this was a-- one other Jew. I don't know what he were cleaning there-- I don't know what he was doing. Anyway, he didn't-- he didn't like him. And he said, take off your clothes-- because before they shot people, their clothes must be safe, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somebody saw, and this prisoner was Lamus. Lamus, this was the name of this Jewish boy. And he [INAUDIBLE], [? she ?] wanted the clothes off. And somebody told Schindler, listen. They wanted to-- and Schindler ran to him. Well, what do you want for him? He didn't work. He didn't work. [INAUDIBLE]. I'm getting you liter of vodka. [INAUDIBLE]. OK, liter. And then Lefkowicz is alive. [LAUGHS] Were there any instances when, say, there were-- did Schindler hold any parties that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Parties? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the film, you saw the parties, wherever they were at-- but me, I don't want to repeat again. Not [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards in the factory? Did you see any guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guard? Factory, around the factory was guard. And in the camp, around, it was-- how you say--

speaker: interviewer
text: Watchtowers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Watching, and machine gun. And there were always light, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the guards ever hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. This-- I had-- in-- in Sudetenland, I had a [? factory ?]. I tell you about [? factory, ?] but I-- OK. When we came to the Sudeten, the Schindler was living with us in one, two rooms, in factory. But he rent in the villa a huge flat. And he told me, listen. I'm going to paint. It was, maybe, 100 meter. You're going to paint my flat, because I'm going to move. I said, all right. But I shouldn't go for myself. SS, SS man. Every day, [INAUDIBLE]-- gone. I walked [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: We going there. And the SS man, maybe was 65-- old man, but Nazi. I [INAUDIBLE], he was there. [INAUDIBLE]. And suddenly, somebody, they brought me lunch-- a soup. They brought me soup. And I was eating. And I was mixing plaster, because if you're mixing plaster, you should wait few minutes, until it's hard. And when I was waiting, he hit me. I said, why you hitting me? You are not working! I said, OK. Not was working, OK. We came in the evening, to the factory. I said, director, he hit me. I said, OK, tomorrow he's not going to be-- I'm going to give another. And he went to commander. And they were friendly. Next-- next day, another old man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They brought me soup. And he said to me, excuse me. Why you are here? I said, I am a Jew. What do you mean you are Jew? Because of Jew, you are here? I said, yes. Do you know when we came here, they told us they are the murderers. We-- we-- we must be careful. They're going to kill us, and-- but you are a Jew. And what is about-- everyone is a Jew. Oh yes. Do you know, my wife is in Breslau. The Russians [INAUDIBLE]. Russians are here, and I am here. What for? Next day, [INAUDIBLE], got a bread, cigarette. This-- this was-- this was before-- before the war. And one bastard-- another one came and said, you know, your uncle is dead. He died. I said, who is the uncle? Roosevelt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in two weeks, it's going to be Churchill, and we going to win the war. Do you remember that-- the soldier's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- SS, no. I know who the commander, I know. Like I tell you, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was the painter. And I was working for the commander at the flat, you know, and the-- the-- for the SS. And they know me there. My master, they talk direct to me, no-- not that other way. And once, I was working central heating, radiators. And I put hand, and suddenly I burned my hand. And we got doctors, Jewish doctors-- Dr. Lefkowicz. And he was the boss. I didn't like him. He didn't-- didn't like me. OK. But see, we to the shower went together. But he was the boss there. I was-- And I got burned. And I went to the-- to the hospital, was-- I said, Lefkowicz, here. You must do something. After 6 o'clock. And it was, maybe, 11 o'clock. I said, listen, buster-- he was-- listen, buster. I'm going to Leipold. Leipold was a commander. Go to-- to the hell. I'm not taking you after 6:00. I say, OK. And I was looking for the Leipold. This was the god, Leipold, it was the commander. And he was fishing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the border of the camp was river. And I saw him fishing with his assistant. And the assistant put him there, [INAUDIBLE], you know. What do you want? I said, [INAUDIBLE]. I said, listen what happen to me. And I want to-- to-- to make it some, and they don't want to take me, because-- Tell them my order. I'm going to him. Thank you very much. Lefkowicz, I got order from Leipold. You should treat me. No! The Leipold here. Why you don't want to-- to help him? You bastard. This is the best painter-- hit him twice, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, hit him, hit him-- and he was shouting at him. [INAUDIBLE] me, of course. But on-- in the evening, we met. I am just the agent. He told me I am the agent, you bloody bastard. You-- you-- I'm going to get the gangrene because-- this was a small thing, though. OK, go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the Emalia factory, did you work in shifts, or did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Yeah, wait a second. One day, the big [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] eating room, eating room for the better people, it was finished, because the brick layer and everyone else-- just to paint. But the paint was a day, what must be finished, because his banquet. The director invite people from-- high-ranking. And he said to me, excuse me. Do me a favor. I want it to be finished. Take people, what do you want-- I said, OK, director. One condition-- I need four or five people. I need food, because I want-- OK, go to the Polish kitchen and tell they what you want. Ah, they give me everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: People was working 10 hours a day. I was working 18 hours a day, no more. Two, three days, four hours sleep-- and I was working four days and four nights, without sleeping. And then was Saturday, should be the banquet. And it was showed in this film, what happened, and the floor, OK? Would you like to explain about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- the-- the floor was concrete. And the Polish master said, I cannot make in the time. I was-- I made beautiful job. [INAUDIBLE], beautiful. But the concrete, you know, concrete. Doesn't look nice. And it was Saturday, the morning. I was dead. [INAUDIBLE] give me advice, what to do. It's not possible. I said, director, is one possibility-- to paint red. OK, make. A powder I got-- at this time, wasn't this paint like today. [INAUDIBLE] emulsion. [? Glue. ?] Everything was OK. And I have a machine to spray. I ran two people like mad, and 12 o'clock was ready, you know. And took out, you know, the-- but it was wet. I didn't know they should come at 7 o'clock. They came 3:00, 4:00-- half past 3:00. And they start to dancing and drinking, and the-- and the-- and the dust, the red dust going up-- they were very happy, because dancing and kissing, and [INAUDIBLE]. But-- but the dust went in their nose, in their [INAUDIBLE] the shirt of white, shirt was red.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get in trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. I was sleeping. It was Sunday in the morning. And they took few of our people to dishes, to wash, you know, because there was luxury, food. And these two people came, and Victor said, if you going to live today, you survive. [INAUDIBLE] what happened? Don't ask me. Everybody's red. [LAUGHS] Well, you know the [INAUDIBLE]. And Schindler came with [? Goth. ?] Come on, meister! Heh, [INAUDIBLE]. Everybody's like idiot. This is our fun. They won't mind this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [? Goth ?] saw the funny side, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was laughing. No, he [INAUDIBLE]. Before he introduced, he knew. He knows me, because he tells me, that's my best painter. I don't know this is sympathy or not. He was so clever, a [INAUDIBLE]. You know what is a [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] He was so clever, you cannot cheat him. It was not possible. He knew everything better. And I was straight honor. If you lied to him, you were finished. [INAUDIBLE]. [SIGHS] What I remember of her, I cannot believe what-- I don't know, I guess-- I cannot believe what I have [INAUDIBLE] from him. I wanted too much for him-- from him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So go on, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- when you first met Schindler, what was he like? You described him before, but were you afraid of him? Did you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you how we start. They took me from-- because we've been together with my wife. And suddenly, they took me, and the wife stay there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know where I am going. After I saw Schindler, we've been together, and I saw how he speak to me, you know. I said, director, you took me from my wife. She's there. I am here, and I want-- I working. Please, send me return, or bring me my wife, here-- stupid, I was stupid. And he said, OK, don't worry. Don't worry. OK, don't worry. And the people, my friends around, saw that I got with Schindler very well. Victor, you got a wife. Yeah, I have a wife. My wife is there. Do something. And every few weeks, I said, director-- don't worry. I'm working. I'm working. And suddenly he said, OK. How many wife you [INAUDIBLE]. Make me a list. And maybe 20, 40, I don't remember. Make me a list. And he said, I am going to try. Never said a word. And after a few weeks, he said, excuse me. Tomorrow, your wife is coming. And she came, with all the wives. And he was there, and he said to-- show me your wife. [INAUDIBLE]. Nice girl. [LAUGHS] She was proud, you know. [INAUDIBLE] Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any dealings that Schindler did?

speaker: interviewee
text: With whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: With-- with Nazi officials, to help the Jews in any way. Did he-- do you remember, did you know of any bribes that he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was so-- he was so-- when the officer came, they were always drunk with [INAUDIBLE]. That's all. I-- I can tell you another fact, with names. I had a friend, is a Roman bull fighter, a Roman bull fighter. And he got connection with Polish people outside. And he-- because the Jews, they get [INAUDIBLE] [? money ?] to the Polish people. And this bull fighter sent a letter through a Polish boy to another Polish, and they caught him. And the Gestapo-- two Gestapo men-- came to our camp. And they wanted a Roman bull fighter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gestapo, [INAUDIBLE] director-- yes sir? And the Gestapo [INAUDIBLE]. I'm looking for-- looking for the bull fighter. Come to-- to me, [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What happened, he said, and they said to him, listen. We've got letter, and it's stupid. You want-- you want from him. He's very-- my best friend, the-- the-- my best worker, what-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bring me that bull fighter here. And somebody brought him. And Herr Schindler said, you bloody bastard. You idiot. You start to [? make it-- ?] [INAUDIBLE] hit him in his face, [INAUDIBLE]. Get out! That's all. You know? This was 100% dead. Was this-- the portrayal of Schindler is that he was a womanizer-- that he used to drink a lot. How true is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Womanizer-- I'm taking-- I'm taking a drink. Excuse me, OK? One minute. You want to be patient with me, now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE]. Mr. Dortheimer's just got up to get another drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a proper womanizer, I'm going to tell you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No one man is a womanizer.

speaker: interviewer
text: One second. You can carry on when I've got the microphone. But I'm holding it.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, OK. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Otherwise, we won't hear you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every man, normal man, is a womanizer.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In my life, if a girl went to my bed, I-- I didn't throw her away. That was Herr Schindler. They were crazy about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just put this on. There we go. Who was crazy?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was so beautiful. He was so manly, so [INAUDIBLE]. They were-- our Jewish-- ah, the director spoke to! He never touched Jewish girl-- never. He was around drunk, he'd kiss a few, you know. But the Gestapo came to him. But a womanizer-- the-- the most beautiful girls was chasing him. Believe me, he never was looking for the girl. His secretary, beautiful girl. She was dead after him. She was in love with him. [INAUDIBLE]. But she was working. At 6 o'clock, she went home. And he went to coffee, you know, between the high people-- always with a woman-- no money, no whores. No whores.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just said-- you mentioned that the Germans approached Schindler about kissing Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said they arrest him. They arrest him. But he got another high-ranking people, what from  me? I was drunk, and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- did they-- did other Germans come to the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure they did. They got high-ranking friends. This is-- they bribed everyone. Listen, how would he keep the factory, with Jews? For the German authority, these people-- we've been the best specialists for producing [INAUDIBLE] for the army. So what you want? They cannot refuse him-- of course, bribery. But officially, we had the best people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his relationship like with Emily, his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emily were very decent girl, innocent girl. And she was in love with him, and she married him. Herr Schindler, before the war, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] first he was a spy for Germany-- was a spy. And he didn't like the work-- sport, beautiful girl, but they married.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was very Catholic, but he not. This was they married. But in the war, he came to Krakow and she visited him one in six months, for one day or two day. So what do you want? He got one girlfriend he got in factory, and another-- Marta-- it was outside factory-- both beautiful girls. And what nationality were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Polish and one was German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what religions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not Jewish, no, no. Not Jewish. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you left-- how did you leave the Emalia factory, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I leave? I was completely independent. Nobody can give me orders. I don't-- I was doing what I like. That's all, but I was still working, still working. You know [? apropos, ?] after this happened, with the floor and whatever-- you know, after-- and director saw what I-- four, five nights I did-- I working, he called me. He gave me beautiful working uniform-- not for Jew, because-- two liter of vodka, and another food. You know what you do with it. I said, I know, Herr Director. I know what to do. I sold, you know, for food. He knew exactly what he was doing. But we'd play, often, cards, often, cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the two of you, or with other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we two just-- not with that. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the-- when the factory was moved, you went to Gross-Rosen, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Plaszow, to Gross-Rosen. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How long were you in Gross-Rosen for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four or five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like to be there, for you, having been so well looked-after in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In factory? What you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the factory, you said you were well looked-after. But then you were transported to this concentration--

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to factory?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, when you left the factory and you were taken to Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, wait a second. We-- we came to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Plaszow, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Plaszow to Gross-Rosen, from Gross-Rosen to Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like in Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't ask me. [LAUGHS] We came, it was autumn. It was afternoon, on the day, and-- in the morning we came there. Afternoon, that where everything take out-- naked, thousand people-- snowing and raining. Naked, a huge place-- and we were like-- like the ships, together, because-- And we was-- they should [INAUDIBLE]-- open there, open there. They were looking for gold, for I don't know. We were waiting for showers, bath. They took hats, everything, everywhere-- hats, you know. My turn-- this was, approximately, 3 o'clock in the morning-- 12 hours in the snow and rain. After the 12 hour, they put us, a group. They-- they shave us, everything. Go to the shower. A beautiful, warm shower-- [INAUDIBLE]. And suddenly they stopped, and they start, like [INAUDIBLE]. It was ice-cold water. You-- you cannot run away, because everywhere. [LAUGHS] After, every three people got one towel. Now you can-- and after, they put us out, run, run, run, run. How-- maybe, I don't know, half mile, to another barracks, naked, wet. One gave you a pull-over. One gave a shirt, and one gave us shoes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You [? go. ?] This was, maybe, 5 o'clock in the morning. Now, OK, we going to sleep. This was a big, huge, about 700 people. It was at least 700. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So one-- I'll show you how we was sleeping. One was-- this is the wall. And one was [? that, ?] and a second here. No, [INAUDIBLE] between. And they start to-- Careful of the wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: To working center-- no central heating, wood. And it was hot. And it was wet. And we are sweating. It was terrible. It is-- was terrible-- no food, beating. In the morning, soup. You get, give you in the-- in the soup One soup, one-- one-- in the-- in the face. [LAUGHS] This was organized. To toilet, this was open toilet, you know, for 100 people-- open. Don't ask me. Don't ask. And exercising, at--
